description: An individual human being, understood as a complete whole with inherent dignity and interconnection to all life. The term emphasizes personhood as both unique individuality and universal humanity.
sound_symbolism: The word begins with 'mi', the sound of individual identity resonating from within - a personal vibration that declares 'I am'. This opens through 'o' into the rounded wholeness of being, acknowledging the complete sphere of personhood. The final 'na' grounds this wholeness in connection, linking the individual to the web of all existence. Together, these sounds journey from inner self through complete being to interconnected presence, capturing the Buddhist understanding of personhood as both uniquely individual and universally connected.
grammatical_notes: Functions exclusively as a noun across languages. Unlike English where 'person' can form compounds that seem verbal (e.g., 'to person the desk'), most languages maintain clear nominal status: Mandarin rén (人), Japanese hito (人), Hindi vyakti (व्यक्ति), Arabic shakhṣ (شخص). In Phi's analytical structure, verbal concepts of personhood would use separate verbs with 'miona' as subject or object.
